[Strategy Overview: Urchin] Urchin is an Action-Attack card from . By itself, Urchin is a very weak handsize attack, but if you can collide it with another Attack, you get a — a powerful Attack and trasher.

[FAQ: Urchin] =
When you play this, you draw a card and get +1 Action, then each other player discards down to four cards in hand.
Players who already have four or fewer cards in hand do not do anything.
While Urchin is in play, when you play another Attack card, before resolving it, you may trash the Urchin.
If you do, you gain a .
The Mercenary comes from the Mercenary pile, which is not in the Supply.
If there are no Mercenaries left you do not gain one.
If you play the same Urchin twice in one turn, such as via , that does not let you trash it for a Mercenary.
If you play two different Urchins however, playing the second one will let you trash the first one.

=

[FAQ: Urchin] =
You do not gain a Mercenary if you trash Urchin some other way.
If you play an Urchin, and then you play a Command variant such as that plays an Attack from the Supply (such as another Urchin), you may still trash your Urchin for a Mercenary.
You trash Urchin and gain a Mercenary before resolving any effects of the other Attack you played, and before deciding whether to use a Way on the Attack instead of resolving its own effects. 
It doesn't matter if the effects of the other Attack card, when they are resolved, will actually end up having any effect on your opponents. For instance, if you play a on your own turn, you can still trash an Urchin from play.
Because you trash the Urchin before resolving the Attack, that means if you trash an Urchin by playing a , you'll gain an . And if you trash an Urchin by playing a , you can gain the Urchin from the trash.
You can order Urchin's ability with other "when you play this, first" abilities (e.g. and ). These still take place before the other players get to React to the Attack you played (with e.g. or ).
If you play Urchin itself as a Way, you can still trash the Urchin when you play an Attack.

[Opening: Urchin] Urchin is a cantrip handsize Attack whose most important function is to provide access to . Since Mercenary is a powerful card offering strong trashing and a better handsize Attack than Urchin, it is often a reason to prioritize gaining Urchin, most frequently in the opening. Mercenary is a strong trasher that not only thins quickly, but also provides a decent amount of cycling, in addition to acting as a handsize attack and making it easy to hit important price points (typically ). However, because it can take several turns to acquire a Mercenary, it can sometimes be better to use another thinner instead. For example, although can only trash one card per play, it can be played much earlier in the game.

[Opening: Urchin] If Mercenary is the best option to trash junk and get deck control, as is frequently the case, your priority is usually to gain one as soon as possible. Since this requires you to play Urchin and another Attack card on the same turn, it's usually best to open with both of these cards and deprioritize other purchases. In practice, the best choice is most often two Urchins, since other Attacks may be more expensive than you can afford in the opening and Urchin's cantrip nature increases the likelihood that you will find your other copy on the same turn. If your Urchins fail to collide on turn 3 or turn 4, you may opt to add a third copy, which further increases the chances of a collision after the second shuffle; however, missing out on the earlier collision is still likely to leave you a few turns behind an opponent who does manage to get Mercenary immediately. Because of how desirable this earlier collision is, it's usually a good idea to look for effects that can facilitate it. You typically can't gain more cards along with your two Urchins on the first two turns, so the effects that are most likely to be useful are those that you can access from the start of the game. Most often, these will take the form of Ways (e.g. ), Events (e.g. ), or Allies (e.g. ) that provide some form of deck control. If you are able to gain a third card, sifters such as are particularly effective.

[Strategy Overview: Urchin] Often it will be worth gaining Urchin independent of whether you want . As a cheap cantrip, it costs relatively little to add it to your deck. As such, it may be a good target for Adventures tokens (e.g. from ), or for bolstering your payload. Urchin can also effectively negate the drawbacks of drawing with or .

[Strategy Overview: Urchin] Later in the game, when you are under Mercenary attack, you will often have to decide between discarding useful cards and keeping junk in hand so you can trash it with your own Mercenary, or keeping your best cards in the hope of achieving more with your turn. Given the high overall value of playing Mercenary, including the chance to counterattack your opponent, keeping cards you want to trash in hand is likely a good move. Sometimes, Mercenary continues to be relevant once you've trashed your starting cards. Most commonly this is due to continued junking attacks, but on rare occasions, the attack is valuable enough and your deck control is strong enough that it's worth gaining junk as fodder for the mandatory trashing effect. A special case is with , which provides unlimited fodder.

[Strategy Overview: Urchin] Obtaining a Mercenary tends to leave you with a single Urchin in your deck, which can continue to be useful as a mild handsize Attack. If it is very important to thin as soon as possible or there is a large amount of junk to trash, it may even be worth converting this into a second Mercenary.

[Strategy Overview: Vagrant] Vagrant is an Action from . It's a cantrip (i.e., it gives +1 Card and +1 Action), and it lets you look at the top card of your deck and draws it if it's one of several types of usually junk cards.

[FAQ: Vagrant] =
You draw a card before revealing your top card.
If the top card of your deck is a , Ruins, Shelter, or Victory card, it goes into your hand; otherwise it goes back on top.

[Strategy Overview: Vagrant] Vagrant is a weak cantrip that can act as non-terminal draw, but only for cards that are typically not very useful to draw, i.e. Victory cards and most kinds of junk. Therefore, in a well-thinned deck, it's most likely to provide no benefit beyond revealing the next card in your deck; and even when it does draw a card, that card is unlikely to help you advance your turn. For these reasons, Vagrant is most impactful in Kingdoms that make cheap cantrips attractive (e.g. to access a Way such as , as a target for vanilla bonus tokens, or for scoring with an effect such as ), although the fact that it generally won't hurt your deck can make it worth picking up a copy or two when the opportunity cost is low, even in the absence of such effects.

[Archetype: Vagrant] If your deck contains cards that Vagrant can draw, it can provide some value in helping you cycle through those more quickly, although because this is unreliable, thinning, sifting, or stronger sources of draw are far better ways to obtain full deck control. It's most likely to be worth getting Vagrant for this purpose in Kingdoms with very weak thinning (or thinning only) or strong junking attacks such as or . Less commonly, it may be useful if support effects such as can allow you to draw with it reliably. Vagrant can also sometimes help you work towards deck control in the early game before you've thinned your junk, or maintain deck control during heavy greening in the endgame. In all of these scenarios, Vagrant can provide significantly more benefit if you can make good use of the cards it can draw. This is sometimes possible with sifting effects that can convert the hand size increase into higher quality cards (e.g. ) or payload (e.g. ), but it also applies in cases where drawing Victory cards is desirable, such as with multi-type cards like , or when you're drawing with .

[Strategy Overview: Vagrant] While Vagrant is far less useful when it reveals a card it cannot draw, this information can inform your deck tracking, or help you make use of effects that care about the top card of your deck, such as .

[Archetype: Wandering Minstrel] Wandering Minstrel is an Action card from . It's one of the few villages whose name refers to an individual, rather than a community or a group of people. It has a filtering ability which helps your engine continue to draw Actions.

[FAQ: Wandering Minstrel] =
First draw a card, then reveal the top three cards of your deck, shuffling your discard pile if there are not enough cards in your deck.
If there still are not enough after shuffling, just reveal what you can.
Put the revealed Action cards on top of your deck in any order, and discard the other cards.
If you didn't reveal any Action cards, no cards will be put on top.

[Strategy Overview: Wandering Minstrel] Wandering Minstrel is one of the strongest villages due to its sifting and deck inspection abilities, which allow you to cycle rapidly through a large amount of junk to find your Action cards more easily, or rearrange your topdecked Action cards in a favorable order when you don't have much junk to sift past.

[Strategy Overview: Wandering Minstrel] In a deck where Wandering Minstrel's only role is to provide +Actions, it should generally be added in a similar manner to other villages, i.e. once you have some terminals in your deck that risk collision and need the extra terminal space. However, because Wandering Minstrel's sifting is so strong, it may sometimes be useful to gain it earlier than you would other villages because it significantly accelerates cycling through your starting cards. This faster cycling is useful in conjunction with Action cards that you want to play early and often, for example Travellers (e.g. ) and Attacks (e.g. ). Similarly, adding more copies of Wandering Minstrel than strictly necessary for terminal space may help with deck control. Even if you have enough draw to draw your entire deck, discarding stop cards (including Victory cards once you start greening) and ordering the Action cards you will draw next increases reliability and offers more flexibility in play order. The reordering effect may also synergize with some effects that depend on the top cards of your deck such as or .

[Strategy Overview: Wandering Minstrel] There are a few tactical concepts to keep in mind when using Wandering Minstrel. 
You'll almost always want to play Wandering Minstrel before your other draw cards, to increase the chance of drawing good cards. 
When playing with multiple Wandering Minstrels, it's also usually a good idea to play other draw cards in between them, to fully utilize the three card search space. For example, if your first Wandering Minstrel draws a cantrip and reveals two desirable Action cards, it's likely best to play the cantrip to draw the first Action card, then another Wandering Minstrel to draw the second and reveal the next three cards. 
Discarding a large number of stop cards, especially weak or dead ones, creates a risk of causing an unfavorable reshuffle with those cards, especially since playing another Wandering Minstrel will trigger one if you have fewer than four cards in your deck. Thus, carefully tracking your deck and discard pile is even more important than usual.

[Strategy Overview: Wandering Minstrel] Wandering Minstrel may be slightly less appealing if the most important cards in your deck are not Action cards. For example, the only payload available might be Treasures such as or , or might be your only gainer. It is worth noting that even if Wandering Minstrel discards those key cards, it may still be helpful in ensuring you draw your entire deck, effectively sifting your payload to the end of your shuffle. Discarding these cards with Wandering Minstrel is therefore most inconvenient if you are not drawing your entire deck, either because it's early in the game or because the draw that would allow you to do so is unavailable, and so it can work poorly with non-Action thinners such as or that you want to play as often as possible in the early game to get deck control. For the same reason, Wandering Minstrel is less helpful than usual if there are Action cards in your deck that you would prefer to sift past: notably, it cannot discard Ruins and can therefore be less effective against Looters than against other junking Attacks.
[Strategy Overview: Advance] Advance is an Event from which enables you to replace an unwanted Action card with another, more desirable, one from the Supply.

[FAQ: Advance] =
If you do not trash an Action, nothing else happens.

[Opening: Advance] To use Advance effectively, you need spare Action cards in your deck to trash and good targets to gain. A source of +Buy is also especially useful, since Advance costs but still comes with the opportunity cost of requiring a Buy. With Advance, you can gain Action cards costing up to , so the majority of all Action cards are available this way (exceptions are the few that cost or more as well as all those with or in their cost). Advance's greatest strength lies in how much easier it makes it to gain otherwise expensive cards, provided that you have Action cards to trash; in some cases, you may even buy Action cards specifically to Advance them. Advance may make it worthwhile to open with a cheap Action instead of a , which can then be Advanced into a more expensive Action card. This can be an effective way to ensure you can gain a or card without adding a long-term stop card to your deck.

[Opening: Advance] Advance fodder generally meets at least one of three criteria:
The Action card is cheap to buy or gain. Ruins are a good example, as they cost , and so if you have sufficient draw and +Buy, it can be economically efficient to gain Ruins (including indirectly via ) with the aim of Advancing them. By the same token, it can be a bad idea to give your opponent free Ruins via or . Generally speaking, gainers such as can easily feed your Advancing needs, though this is most useful when those gainers cannot just gain the card that you ultimately want, requiring you to use Advance as a stepping stone. For this reason, more restricted gainers such as gainers and tend to work well with Advance, as you can convert what they gain into other, more useful Action cards.
The Action card is no longer useful in your deck. The in Shelters games is a prime example for this, and a good target for trashing in the opening. Other Actions to trash can include those that have overstayed their welcome (e.g. if you used to thin your deck initially), or that you primarily gained for some on-gain benefit. is an especially strong example of the latter: as long as you have an Action card in your hand to Advance, you can gain a Cavalry, which draws you 2 cards and gives you the Buy back. This makes it much easier to quickly draw your deck. Furthermore, once you have drawn your deck, you will draw the Cavalries that you have been gaining, and these can be Advanced into more Cavalries for an autopile, or into more Action cards.
The Action card gives you some benefit when trashed. is a very strong example of this, as you can repeatedly Advance a single copy of it, as long as you have Buys. can also be good Advance fodder, as it gives you a Villager and a Coffers in addition to the card you Advanced into.

[Strategy Overview: Aqueduct] Aqueduct is a Landmark from . It acts kind of like a Gathering card, accumulating tokens as players gain Treasures, and then giving them to a player when they gain a Victory card.

[FAQ: Aqueduct] =
If you gain a card that is both a Treasure and a Victory card, such as , you can resolve the abilities in either order.

=

[FAQ: Aqueduct] =
Aqueduct only puts on and , but if you gain a different Treasure that has on its pile, you'll move to Aqueduct.
For example: If you have and then gain a , you'll move from the pile onto Aqueduct.
Action Supply piles can have on them due to . If you rotate to e.g. , then gaining it would move to Aqueduct.
Aqueduct can move from non-Supply piles, for example if Farmers' Market is the pile gained with and you have bought Capitalism.
If the card you gain is both a Treasure and an Action (because of Capitalism, or because it's ), you move each to Aqueduct and Defiled Shrine. If there's only on the pile, you choose which Landmark to move it to.
If you gain a Treasure from somewhere other than the Supply (e.g. gaining a Gold from the trash with ), you'll still move the tokens from that card's pile.
If gaining a Victory card causes you to gain a Treasure (e.g. makes you gain a Gold), you can move the from the Treasure pile to Aqueduct, and then immediately take them.

[Strategy Overview: Aqueduct] Because people tend to start with and may be either easy to obtain or actually valuable as payload, there is a high probability that there will land some tokens on Aqueduct. Just like , this may incentivise a slightly sooner greening stage or at least a single gain of a good victory card, maybe an early . 
There is also the possibility for a gimmicky "megaturn", such as overpaying a lot for masterpiece, to then put all of the 8 points on Aqueduct and then buying a single or even a feodom, if available. This beats out the point gain of a province and helps the deck more, if your deck can handle the 10 stop cards. This strategy, more than any other megaturn suffers from 4 or 5 player games, because chances are high somebody else already took the points before the megaturn can happen.

[Strategy Overview: Archive] Archive is an Action-Duration card from . It non-terminally increases your handsize on future turns, while letting you choose the order in which you'll draw the three cards it gives you.

[FAQ: Archive] =
You look at three cards, and get one now, one next turn, and one the turn after that.
Put the set-aside cards under Archive.
If you play two Archives, they get separate sets of cards.
If you an Archive, keep the sets of cards separate; you get one from each each turn.
If there are fewer than three cards, just set aside what you can, and Archive will run out of cards faster and still be discarded the turn it has no cards left.

=

[FAQ: Archive] =
A on an Archive remains in play until the Archive is discarded, even if there are not enough cards left in your deck to sustain a second Archive effect for the full three turns.

[Strategy Overview: Archive] As a sort of mash up of , , and , Archive can provide duration draw, deck thinning, and next-turn set up all in one package, however it does each of these things very differently compared to other cards that provide these benefits individually. This often means that on boards with better draw or thinning, Archive will be passed over in favor of more reliable and more typical options (especially given its high price which competes with other powerful s). But under the right circumstances when draw or thinning are lacking, Archive can humbly step in as a quiet yet versatile hero. By learning how to recognize boards where Archive is useful and where it's less so, we can get better at noticing when key deck components are missing, and capitalize on “unconventional” solutions.

[Strategy Overview: Archive] =
As mentioned above, Archive provides three essential benefits: duration draw, deck thinning, and next-turn set up. We'll briefly examine each piece and discuss how Archive is different from other cards that fulfill similar roles.
[Strategy Overview: Archive] = Learning to work with what you've got =
[Strategy Overview: Arena] Arena is a Landmark from . It holds a finite number of tokens, which players take from if they keep an unplayed Action in hand to discard at the start of their Buy phase.

[FAQ: Arena] =
With it is possible for your Buy phase to start twice or more in a turn; you can make use of Arena each time.

=

[FAQ: Arena] =
You can discard an Action card even if there's no more on this, which can be useful with cards like that do something special when discarded.

[Strategy Overview: Arena] Arena is a relatively low-impact Landmark. are usually of minimal importance early in the game, and even small improvements to your deck quality will have a much larger cumulative effect over the long term compared to an early lead in . Discarding Action cards to Arena that you would otherwise play is therefore usually not worth the cost until later in the game. However, in weaker Kingdoms with limited scoring options (e.g., those where you can only gain one per turn), Arena's may be more important than playing a weak Action card.
 
In games with Shelters, Arena will frequently have no impact at all, as players will be able to discard their to Arena each shuffle, usually resulting in Arena points being quickly split at per player.
 
Arena slightly lessens the cost of building a deck that is overterminaled because if you have terminal collision you can at least still discard the unplayed Action card for some benefit. Arena also lessens the drawback of receiving Ruins; Ruins count as Action cards but are too weak to be worth playing in most cases and are therefore prime targets for Arena.
 
Arena synergizes with , which returns to your next hand when discarded, allowing it to be discarded to Arena every turn for little cost. Arena can also be used to activate . In these cases, it may be desirable to discard to Arena even if no remain available.
[Strategy Overview: Bandit Fort] Bandit Fort is a Landmark from . It penalizes players with negative for each and they have.

[FAQ: Bandit Fort] =
For example with 3 and 1 , you would get .
Scores can go negative.

[Archetype: Bandit Fort] Bandit Fort is a Landmark that heavily disincentivizes relying on and for payload. As a result, Action-based payload (such as or ) or strong alternative Treasures (such as or ) will be more appealing. In the absence of other reasonable payload options, Bandit Fort might even increase the viability of cards like that are usually weak and expensive to gain in comparison to the basic Treasures. Depending on what alternatives are available, Bandit Fort may make a weak engine using subpar payload such as a better option than a money strategy that has to suffer a large penalty. Bandit Fort's presence may also slightly increase the appeal of skipping weak thinning in favor of building a deck that can draw Coppers instead (for example with ).

[Opening: Bandit Fort] Silver and Gold can still be worth using even in the presence of Bandit Fort. If there is no alternative payload available, or the other options are exceedingly weak, the penalty is somewhat negated, as both players will need to obtain Silver and/or Gold in order to build effective decks at all. Even if other payload is available, it may not be accessible or viable in the opening, and so an early Silver or two might help you hit important early price points (e.g. ) and build faster than you could otherwise. Regardless of the alternatives available, because the penalty is only a concern at the end of the game, trashing your problematic Treasures before that point mitigates the downside of temporarily using them. is particularly notable for this: if you have a spare Buy, you can remove all Silver and Gold from your deck at the end of your final turn, with no downside. This also means that Silver and Gold gainers can still act as good providers of fodder for effects like as the Treasures won't remain in your deck for long.

[Strategy Overview: Bandit Fort] Bandit Fort can also affect the effectiveness of non-payload aspects of the Kingdom. 
Methods of scoring that rely on Silver and Gold such as and are often negated. 
Mandatory Silver and Gold gainers that you would play for other purposes now have an on-play penalty that may make them less appealing. Examples include advancing your Traveller by playing or playing to try and junk your opponent.
[Strategy Overview: Banquet] Banquet is an Event from . It lets you get and cards at a discount, with the penalty of also junking your deck with extra .

[FAQ: Banquet] =
You can do this even if the pile is empty.

[Strategy Overview: Banquet] Banquet is a weak Event that provides a cheap way to gain a card costing or less at the cost of taking two along with it. Junking yourself is not often worthwhile, but Banquet can be very helpful if you have a way of getting rid of the Coppers easily or if they are actively beneficial. Banquet can also allow you to pile out more cheaply.

[Opening: Banquet] Infrequently, you can use Banquet to supply you with powerful cards early and often if you have a good way of trashing the Coppers before they can interfere with your deck control. For example, if you have a (which lets you trash one junk card per gain) or in hand, you can neutralize them readily; similarly, you might use Banquet to obtain strong payload cards cheaply before buying . In general, however, opening with Banquet for a card is counterproductive, as the advantage of playing the a shuffle early is outweighed by the Coppers causing you to see that more infrequently and simultaneously dragging down the average quality of your deck.

[Archetype: Banquet] Sometimes Banquet's Copper gaining increases its appeal. If extra net gains are difficult to come by, you may be able to make use of the Coppers, e.g. as fodder for a card like to trash. In other cases, certain effects form powerful synergies with Banquet by making Copper specifically a valuable card to gain. In particular, combined with repeated Banquet purchases can generate enough Coffers to start reliably buying a single per turn rather early in the game. Banquet also works very well with a -based money strategy, since it not only makes this Treasure easier to gain but also provides an increase in money density via the flood of Coppers that is somewhat helpful for this strategy; for similar reasons.

[Strategy Overview: Banquet] Occasionally, Banquet can also help with scoring, even though you can't use it to gain Victory cards. It adds extra cards to your deck quickly, which is beneficial late in the game when points are relevant. The Coppers can also provide , e.g. with or .

[Strategy Overview: Baths] Baths is a Landmark from . It holds a finite number of tokens, which players take from when they end a turn without having gained any cards.

If a player ends the game on a turn without gaining any cards (such as by playing a and emptying the pile), they will still get the from this, as the end-of-game check happens after the turn is ended.
Exchanging a card does not count as gaining a card. As an example, if your entire turn is playing a , trashing two cards, and exchanging for a , you will get the from Baths.

[Strategy Overview: Baths] Baths is a low-impact Landmark that is often not worth prioritizing. Gaining cards is important for both building and greening, and is a relatively small amount and frequently not worth passing an entire turn for.

[Archetype: Baths] The one notable exception to this is with in the Kingdom, as a turn that only buys Salt the Earth now scores instead of . By aiming to repeatedly buy Salt the Earth, you can in theory rush down the pile. This can be countered by contesting the Baths' by Salting , and thus usually only works if the Baths' are not contested.

[Strategy Overview: Baths] Other than Salt the Earth, Baths' can be relevant as a small tactical bonus when you can do something useful without gaining a card. Examples of this include: 
Playing a thinner that trashes most of your hand, e.g. . This is fairly likely to happen, as mass thinners are often strong enablers of deck control, and the opportunity cost of Baths is low or zero.
Playing a Duration card and otherwise passing your turn, e.g. with or . This is more likely to happen with junking Attacks, which often weaken your ability to gain or buy cards and thus lower the opportunity cost of Baths.
Buying an Event such as , perhaps to set up a game-ending pileout next turn, and winning with the lead from taking Baths.

[Strategy Overview: Battlefield] Battlefield is a Landmark from . It holds a finite number of tokens, which players take from when they gain a Victory card.

[FAQ: Battlefield] =
You take the whether you bought the Victory card or gained it another way.

[Archetype: Battlefield] Battlefield rewards you for gaining Victory cards earlier than your opponents do, which encourages you to start greening slightly earlier than you otherwise would. While the from Battlefield are generally not significant enough to make a huge difference to your strategy, they can offer increased support to certain types of deck. Specifically, decks that want to be gaining Victory cards early anyways are likely to perform more competitively than usual. This is perhaps most apparent in the case of . Decks that rely on Rebuild are typically very good at ending the game quickly, but do not score a large number of points in doing so. Therefore, the points you will naturally pick up from Battlefield become very important, and make Rebuild decks much harder to beat. Similarly, decks that are attempting to rush a cheap pile of Victory cards, for example with and , will also be significantly boosted by Battlefield. Furthermore, Victory cards that you would want to add to your deck for other purposes (such as for the trashing, or as an engine component) become more valuable. Finally, decks that are reliant on for draw may want to add Victory cards anyways, and thus benefit from Battlefield.

[Archetype: Battlefield] In the absence of strategies that give you another reason to gain Victory cards especially early, Battlefield usually encourages whatever strategy would start buying first. Typically, this means that Battlefield will encourage a simpler money strategy over a more complicated engine which would green later. However, the reward in for this is small, so this really only matters if the choice between these strategies would have been close anyways.

[Strategy Overview: Battlefield] Occasionally, it can be reasonable to buy an simply to contest the Battlefield points. Since adding an Estate to a deck is usually a very bad idea outside of the endgame, this should typically only be done if you have already achieved deck control, can potentially trash the Estate easily, have nothing better to buy with , or the swing of an opponent getting the rather than you will matter.

[Strategy Overview: Capital] Capital generates no on net, instead effectively offering you a loan: you get to spend but later you must pay off before you can buy new cards. Therefore, this Treasure should not be regarded as directly increasing your deck's -based payload, except on your final turn. Before that, it has various other uses:
to invest in adding deck control or payload that will quickly pay dividends on the incurred
to smooth out your across turns, or spike a large amount of on a particular turn
as a source of +Buy
Capital would be an expensive and inconvenient option if used solely for the extra Buy, but fortunately this combines well with its other functions.

[Archetype: Capital] Smoothing may be especially helpful when you can't reliably draw your deck and your hands generate variable amounts of , e.g. in a money strategy. For instance, you could use plus Capital to buy a , taking a net ; then, if you generate next turn, you can pay off the and still afford another Province. Even if you have a reliable engine, the produced by Capital can be used to carry spare over between turns in a similar way to Coffers. Spiking, in contrast, is helpful when you need a lot of on a single turn for a high-value one-off purchase (e.g. ), when this will accelerate your deck's capabilities and is worth a subsequent turn spent mainly paying off . At the end of the game, Capital does contribute true payload: for instance, you might use it to help you buy the last three even though your deck can only sustainably afford two per turn, since that you take on your final turn never has any negative effect.

This can be achieved by playing Capital multiple times while discarding it from play only once (with , , or , which generates or for the same as usual) or not at all (with , which generates a one-off and no ). Another synergy is with , which can repeatedly set Capitals aside instead of discarding them, producing no . 
Capital can also work well in kingdoms with , where burying yourself in might hurt your opponent, who must pay it off before buying anything whenever they Possess you.

[Archetype: Chariot Race] Chariot Race is a cantrip that can act as a Peddler variant and source of alt-VP, although it does so only unreliably. Whether you activate its bonuses is generally dependent on a mix of luck and the composition of your deck relative to that of your opponent's. Because is worth significantly more than , Chariot Race is typically more important as a source of than as -generating payload, and its relevance in a Kingdom is mostly tied to how likely it is to activate, how frequently you can play it, and how much the extra scoring matters. It therefore tends to provide the best value when you can thin your starting cards (or control which cards Chariot Race reveals) and consistently play many copies, potentially scoring as much as a each turn. If scoring is otherwise difficult, for example in a single-gain Kingdom, a small difference in scored using Chariot Race may be decisive. 
 
Because your chance of revealing a winning card with Chariot Race is dependent on the average price of the cards in your deck, raising this average is particularly important in a game where you anticipate that Chariot Race will play a significant role. This may involve adding expensive cards to your deck, but trashing your cheap starting junk is usually the more critical component and may be an even higher priority than usual. Getting rid of cards such as and is particularly important, as they are both guaranteed never to win on your own turn and a potentially major liability if your opponent is able to play multiple Chariot Races at once while one of these cards is on top of your deck. Because gaining many Chariot Races is likely to push the average price of cards in a deck towards , cards costing or can become similarly risky as the game proceeds. This also means that, particularly in a mirror, winning the Chariot Race split can be slightly counterproductive: although you get more plays of Chariot Race, you might not win many comparisons if your opponent is building by gaining many or cards. For this reason, it's usually best to prioritize building a strong deck in the early and midgame, adding Chariot Race later or when the opportunity cost is low.

[Strategy Overview: Chariot Race] Effects that synergize with Chariot Race generally help in one of two ways: either by manipulating your own deck, or by manipulating your opponent's. 
It's generally more efficient to have control over the top card of your opponent's deck via an effect such as , as doing so can remove very expensive cards (such as Province) and set up a single card for multiple Chariot Races. If you have access to such an effect and will play multiple Chariot Races, it is typically best to play one of them to learn what the top card of their deck is, and then decide whether to change it. Once you do find a good target, it's likely best to avoid manipulating your opponent's deck until after you play all of your Chariot Races; for example, this might mean delaying drawing with . 
Deck inspectors are the most straightforward way to set up your deck for Chariot Race comparisons, with those that can order multiple cards (e.g. ) being the most effective. Other ways to set up cards may include topdecking them (e.g. with or ) or revealing them (e.g. with ), but may be less appealing because they only work for a single Chariot Race. One potentially effective way to set up multiple cards is by using sifters: if you can draw most of your deck, you can set up your discard pile with your most expensive cards, trigger a reshuffle, and draw through it with Chariot Races.

[Strategy Overview: Chariot Race] Chariot Race can be slightly less productive in Kingdoms where players are likely to have many cards with unusual cost cards (i.e. or ), since it can be difficult to win comparisons with or against them. For example, if your opponent has several , you may need to invest in a way to change the top card of their deck.

[Archetype: Charm] Charm is a flexible Treasure that gives you two options for planning out your gains during your Buy phase. The two options shape Charm into either a with +Buy, or a gainer limited by the economy you've amassed this turn. Both options allow you to gain an extra card, which accelerates the building of your deck. Charm is best in Kingdoms where there are multiple desirable cards at the same pricepoint. A common case of this is two cards, such as and . In this case, Charm is essentially generating and a +Buy, provided you can hit without using Charm for . Charm is usually best in engines that can support the large hand sizes needed to generate the required to hit those pricepoints without Charm's help.

[Opening: Charm] Charm is typically best in the mid- and late-game. In the opening and early-game when your deck is generating less , you're less likely to hit the desired pricepoints to use Charm as a gainer. If there are multiple desirable low-cost cards, however, Charm's +Buy can be useful. In the endgame, it may be possible to gain multiple at once and possibly pileout by buying another with multiple Charms in play. This may be particularly relevant in Kingdoms with .

[Strategy Overview: Charm] Charm is less powerful in Kingdoms with other sources of +Buy or gains, or without multiple desirable cards at the same pricepoint. A limiting factor of Charm's extra gain option is that the gained card must be different than the one you bought. So at , for instance, you won't be able to add two . However, with more than one Charm played, you can gain multiple copies of the same card. If you want multiple , for instance, with from other sources, simply buy another card (such as Charm itself), and each Charm in play can gain a Laboratory. Another weakness of Charm is that it's reliant on the rest of your deck hitting the pricepoints necessary for it to be useful—in inconsistent decks Charm is less likely to be a good gainer, and in decks that need to use stop cards to generate , Charm exacerbates draw issues. Charm may also be worse with cards that have buy order considerations, such as .

[Strategy Overview: Charm] Charm has a notable synergy with . Buying a Prince allows you to gain another card, which could be a . This can become an efficient way of scoring in a megaturn, in which you play many Charms and then gain as many Provinces as Charms you've played.

[Strategy Overview: City Quarter] City Quarter is an Action card from . It is a village that draws cards only if you have Actions in your hand already. It is one of the cards from Empires with a cost.

[FAQ: City Quarter] =
Cards with multiple types that include Action, such as , are Actions.

[Archetype: City Quarter] City Quarter is a powerful draw card that rewards you for building a deck with high Action density. In a large hand with lots of Actions it can draw 10 or even 20 cards, but if you draw it in a hand without any other Actions, it won't draw anything. It also provides a village effect, though due to how expensive it is, it's inefficient if you're buying it primarily as a village. Because it relies on having hands with many Action cards, it is useful almost exclusively in engines.

[Strategy Overview: City Quarter] City Quarter is a poor buy in the early game when your deck has few Action cards in it. As a rule of thumb, try to avoid buying City Quarter until you expect it to draw at least two cards.

[Strategy Overview: City Quarter] City Quarter's cost presents a unique set of incentives as far as when to buy it. Buying City Quarter right before you shuffle with some left over is often a good play. Taking a City Quarter as your last buy is also generally good since you can leverage the to get good cards in your deck sooner than if City Quarter cost .

[Archetype: City Quarter] Since City Quarter likes decks consisting mostly of Action cards, trashing your starting cards and using Actions rather than Treasures as payload are beneficial. Start-of-turn draw is especially helpful, since it makes it easier to kick off with a big City Quarter draw. Similarly, methods of seeding good cards into the starting hand (for example with or ) can snowball one good turn into many more good turns. These reliability boosters can make City Quarter reliable as a main source of draw even if the board lacks trashing or Action card payload. Conversely, handsize attacks and curser attacks can make it more difficult to get value out of City Quarter. Looters aren't nearly as much of a problem, and in some cases are even friendly. On boards where City Quarter isn't reliable as the primary source of draw, but you're still building an engine, it can be a good buy later on when you are already drawing a large proportion of your deck.

[Strategy Overview: City Quarter] Since City Quarter often provides large amounts of overdraw, it is especially good with gainers and other ways of taking advantage of overdraw. is a notable example, since playing a City Quarter using Way of the Chameleon can generate large amounts of economy. Another powerful synergy is with , which gives a coffer every time it is revealed by one of your City Quarters.

[Strategy Overview: Crown] Crown is an Action-Treasure card from . It is a Throne Room variant that double-plays either Treasures or Actions depending on which phase of your turn it's played in.

[FAQ: Crown] =
If you play this in your Action phase, you play an Action card from your hand, then play the same card again; this does not use up any extra Actions you have.
If you play this in your Buy phase, you play a Treasure from your hand, then play it again; this does not use up any Actions at all.
Crown can be used to play another Crown in either your Action or Buy phase, causing you to either play two more Actions twice each, or two more Treasures twice each.
If you play Crown in your Action phase via something that lets you play Treasures (like from ), Crown will still play an Action card twice.
Crown is always both an Action and a Treasure, regardless of what phase it is.

=

[FAQ: Crown] =
If you have abilities to resolve at the start of your turn, it is considered part of your Action phase, and you cannot end it. This means if you play Crown at the start of your turn (with e.g, ), you cannot skip to your Buy phase and play a Treasure twice.
Crown counts as both an Action and a Treasure for all purposes, regardless of when you play it. If you play a Crown in your Action phase, it's still a Treasure and will be trashed from play if you buy . If you play it in your Buy phase, it's still an Action and will reduce the cost of . And so on.
Crown can be played as a Way even if it's played in the Buy phase.
If you somehow manage to play Crown when it's neither your Action phase nor your Buy phase, you can't play any card from your hand twice. (This could happen if you gain it in the Night phase (with e.g. ) or Clean-up (with e.g. ) and then play it with .) The same is true if you play it on someone else's turn by means such as gaining a .

[Archetype: Crown] Crown is a Throne Room variant that can double-play Treasures as well as Action cards. As a slightly more expensive that offers a minor increase in flexibility, it is a strong addition to most engines, as it can amplify your draw and payload as needed while acting as a village simultaneously; however, its relatively high price can make it more difficult to acquire in bulk, and so it's especially appealing in Kingdoms where you can gain it easily, e.g. with or .

[Strategy Overview: Crown] Compared to Throne Room, Crown provides added flexibility in its ability to double-play Treasures as well as Action cards. Gaining Crown specifically for this purpose is usually not the best use of resources, as it typically only nets the same value as another copy of that Treasure. However, it can be a good use for a spare Crown if you are overdrawing and didn't need it to double-play a draw card this turn, or as a fallback plan on a dud turn where you draw Crown without a good Action phase target. Situationally, Crowning a strong Treasure such as or can be a good move, and there are also a few cases in which Treasures offer specific synergy as Crown targets. Notably, can be a good target, as you play it twice to spike a large amount of while only incurring the once.

[Strategy Overview: Crown] The fact that Crown has the Treasure type provides some additional benefits even when you're not using it to Crown Treasures. Most importantly, this means you can put it into play during resolution of or in order to double-play an Action card without using up terminal space. Crown's additional typing may also be relevant with effects such as or . Less frequently, it could be useful to play Crown using a Way during the Buy phase in order to do so without using terminal space; this is not typically the most effective use for your Crown, but it may occasionally be relevant, e.g. to set one aside for next turn using . The downside of this feature is that you cannot Crown Action cards during your Buy phase, so it cannot offer the same lines of play that Throne Room can with certain effects such as and . Using Crown as a village is further very difficult when under the attack of , devaluing it for that purpose.

[Strategy Overview: Crown] Like other Throne Room variants, Crown can be a good target for bonus tokens, particularly the (e.g. from ), which increases your chance of finding a good target for Crown to play. Some of these tokens might also have value if you play Crown during your Buy phase.

[Strategy Overview: Delve] Delve is an Event from . Its presence in a game essentially makes cheaper and not require a Buy to buy.

For example, if you have , you can Delve, then Delve, then buy a card for .

[Opening: Delve] Delve can get you 3 Silvers in the opening, giving you significantly better odds of hitting 5, 6, and 5 twice on turns 3 and 4 than most other openings. This is especially relevant in games where you can use the extra Silvers profitably later, either via trash for benefit or because the board is weak and Silver is likely to remain an above-median card in your deck into the endgame. Delve is fast enough that it can occasionally enable a money strategy to beat a weak engine, but don't let it trick you into not building a deck if there's a deck to be built.
[Strategy Overview: Delve] Delve is notably strong with , and can sometimes even be played as a monolithic strategy. This has been benchmarked as follows:

[Strategy Overview: Dominate] Dominate is an Event from . It gives the player a plus tokens, for a total of . It has the most expensive cost of any card or Event in the game, and can be thought of as the next step up on the basic Victory card chain from .

[FAQ: Dominate] =
This does nothing once the pile is empty.

=

[FAQ: Dominate] =
If you exchange the with a or a , you still get . But if you a player and make them buy Dominate, no one gets .

[Archetype: Dominate] Dominate is a powerful source of alt-VP with very good efficiency and density (i.e. a large amount of scored compared to Buys used, spent, and number of stop cards added to your deck). Although Dominate relies on the pile, it is similar in effect to : there is a higher density and larger total amount of available, and its presence tends to require and reward additional building. In order to buy Dominate, you need a deck with both strong draw and strong payload, likely in the form of an engine; in some Kingdoms, building one may not be possible, and so buying Dominate may not be feasible.

[Strategy Overview: Dominate] The higher density and greater total amount of available has some important effects. Most crucially, you can easily come from behind using Dominate against an alternative method of scoring. This is because it will generally outscore most other sources of : two Dominates score as many points as three Colonies and also provide more than the complete pile of .

[Archetype: Dominate] Dominate relies on the Province pile, so it behaves differently from typical alt-VP strategies. In particular, you can sometimes opt to buy two or more Provinces instead of Dominate; while this may be a less efficient way to score, it brings the end of the game much closer and helps you threaten to conclude it. By the same token, if buying Dominate would allow an opponent who has also been buying Dominate to simply buy out the remaining Provinces and win, you may be in a potential Duchy dancing situation. Conversely, unlike a scoring strategy that relies solely on Provinces, Dominate provides over half of its in VP tokens, so you can potentially trash the Province and still keep a significant amount of . This is most relevant with trash-for-benefit cards like or , which can trash a Province to help you get your next Dominate. Milling effects via Remodel variants can also be useful.

[Strategy Overview: Dominate] There are several interactions that arise because Dominate is an Event rather than a Victory card. On the negative side, cost reduction (such as that of ) does not reduce the cost of Dominate. On the other hand, buying Dominate does not entail buying a card, which negates attacks such as and allows you to sidestep the drawbacks of .

[Strategy Overview: Dominate] and allow you to buy Dominate without lowering the Province pile, delaying the end of the game. This is most useful against opponents using alternate forms of scoring. However, this strategy may fall behind an opponent who also buys Dominate but keeps the Provinces, assuming they can continue to draw their deck. In the endgame, exchanging the Province will still be important when lowering the pile would allow your opponent to empty it on their turn.

[Strategy Overview: Donate] Donate is an Event from . It is a trasher with a Debt cost. It is perhaps the strongest trasher in the game, able to trash any number of cards from the buyer's entire deck.
[Strategy Overview: Donate] Donate was revised in 2022 to change its timing: Donate's effect originally took place between turns, but now it happens as the first thing at the start of your next turn.

[Strategy Overview: Donate] =
Donate happens before anything else that happens at the start of your turn, such as taking a card from .
Effects that happen due to trashing cards (such as ) will happen before you shuffle.

[FAQ: Donate] =
The start of your turn is during your Action phase.
If trashing cards draws you cards that were somehow not already in your hand, it is too late to trash those, and you will shuffle them into your deck.
Anything that cares about shuffling (such as ) happens when you shuffle your hand into your deck.
You only shuffle your hand into your deck; any cards that get into your discard pile (such as with or when reacting with ) stay there.
You draw 5 cards no matter how many you had in hand before Donate.
Donate doesn't happen if the game ends first.
If you use to control a turn where Donate happens, you choose what they trash, and those cards are set aside until after they draw their next hand.

[Strategy Overview: Donate] Donate is a centralizing Event that rewards calculation and careful planning by offering massive acceleration towards clean decks very early, with relatively little room for shuffle luck to matter. Donate's strength arises from the fact that it's the fastest trasher in almost any Kingdom and often makes it very easy to build a strong deck from a very clean starting position. Donate's cost is high, but because it is Debt it can be paid off over multiple turns.

[Opening: Donate] In Kingdoms with Donate it's often possible to plan out the first 4 or 5 turns precisely. The main question is usually on which turn to buy it, and the answer is often dependent on your opening split and what the Kingdom offers. Generally speaking, Donate lessens the need for early trashers, draw, and other forms of deck control, and allows you to essentially start building a deck with payload cards (especially gainers) first. Some common patterns are as follows:
Turn 1 Donate: This is a less common option because it usually involves keeping 5 , but it can be a viable strategy to Donate on turn 1 to a deck of 5 Coppers, pay off the debt on turn 2, buy an important card such as on turn 3, and hope it doesn't bottomdeck on turn 4.
Turn 2 Donate: One of the more common options. Usually bought in order to benefit from having one opening buy in deck, and to avoid having to rely on drawing a good turn 3 hand. An example of this is opening with /Donate on a opening split, then Donating down to 3 Coppers and the Silver in order to buy a on turn 4. Another example on a opening split is opening with on turn 1, Donating on turn 2 to the Ironworks and 3 Coppers, gaining a Silver with the Ironworks and paying off Debt on turn 3, then gaining another Silver and buying a on turn 4.
Turn 3 or Turn 4 Donate: Also among the more common options. Usually taken in order to benefit from having two opening buys in deck, but is more dependent on shuffle luck than Donating earlier. Depending on which cards you draw on turn 3, you can decide whether to wait until turn 4 to Donate. If, for example, you open with and with the goal of buying and gaining multiple Stockpiles and also buying Donate on turn 3, but instead draw 2 Coppers and 3 , you'll likely prefer to wait until turn 4 to Donate, leaving you a turn or more behind an opponent who adopts a similar strategy but gets luckier draws.
Donating later: It is very rare to Donate for the first time later than turn 4, as this would mean wasting time drawing through your bad starting cards. It is not that rare, however, to Donate a second time later in the game to clean up either leftover Coppers or junk from junking attacks.
Skipping Donate entirely: It is very rare to fully skip Donate, as it's often just a faster way of getting to an ideal deck than the alternatives. Kingdoms with and therefore are possible exceptions, as the Goat can thin decks fairly quickly without needing to take on .

[Opening: Donate] Donate works well with cards that can take advantage of the otherwise unproductive turns that you spend paying off the Debt. Gainers that you can open with (e.g. ) are the most common examples of such. Attacks are also possible candidates, with handsize attacks such as significantly hurting your opponents, who likely have very good 5-card hands and no draw at this stage of the game. Travellers are also good options for this, as you can start advancing them quickly up the line.

[Strategy Overview: Donate] When you bought , Donate can change your deck's final contents if you buy it at the end of the final game turn. It can be used to score in Kingdoms with Landmarks such as , , , and .

[Strategy Overview: Donate] A fairly rare tactical use for Donate is using it to skip bad shuffles (e.g., one with all terminal draw and no villages), as Donate is an expensive way to guarantee you shuffle almost your whole deck.
[Synergy: Donate] - Combo: Donate and Market Square

[Synergy: Donate] can be opened alongside Donate for an early economy boost.

[FAQ: Emporium] =
Since the attempt to gain (and thus, the check to see how many Actions are in play) has "when-gain" timing, if another effect with "when-gain" timing brings more Actions into play (such as being called), the player can choose to resolve the effect bringing the Actions into play first, delaying the attempted gain until after those effects are resolved. This can lead to instances where when an Emporium is first gained, fewer than 5 Actions are in play, but the player still gets , since they have chosen to do so after bringing a sufficient amount of Actions into play. For example, with only four Actions in play, a player could buy an Emporium, choose to call Duplicate first, then gain (since they now have 5 Actions in play), then another from the Duplicated Emporium.

[Archetype: Encampment] Encampment is an Action card from . It is a cheap variant of — a village that also increases handsize, a very powerful engine component — but you only get to use it once unless you have a or in hand. It belongs to a split pile; five copies of Encampment sit on top of five copies of Plunder.

[FAQ: Encampment] =
Revealing a or is optional.
When you return Encampment to the Supply, it goes on top of its pile, potentially covering up a Plunder.

=

[FAQ: Encampment] =
If you cannot return Encampment to the Supply (e.g. you bought it from the ), then if it is set aside it will stay there but will still count as one of your cards at the end of the game.
If you somehow play this at the start of Clean-up, and it sets itself aside, it will immediately return to the Supply.

[Archetype: Encampment] Encampment has three defining characteristics:
Like , Encampment combines non-terminal draw and a village into a single card, which is very powerful. Draw and village effects form the backbone of many engines, and combining the two into a single cheap card reduces your odds of dudding and speeds up the building process. 
If you cannot collide it with a or , it is a one-shot. In the early game, it can be fine to gain Encampment and play it in this way if you otherwise miss the pricepoint you want, as the early cycling and draw can help you afford and play other important cards in a manner similar to . In games with limited gains, however, it may be wiser to wait until you can retain the Encampment. In some Kingdoms, it may be possible to play your Encampments using Ways in order to get some value out of your early Encampments. Later in the game, you usually have more deck control and retaining Encampments is more feasible, and doing so means less wasted resources regaining them. Because Encampment is collision-dependent, you'll usually want to get some deck control before adding a Gold or Plunder to your deck, usually by thinning your starting cards. Otherwise, you'll add an expensive stop card to your deck without much guarantee of keeping your Encampments. Effects such as and , or duration draw such as , can also help ensure these collisions early in the game. Because It's difficult to uncover Plunders without retaining Encampments (except with ), Gold is typically used initially to retain Encampments. As such, effects such as on-buy ability and Gold-gainers such as are worth considering in Kingdoms with Encampment, as they lower the cost of acquiring a Gold, as is spiking for an early Gold if you can thin quickly. In cases where you do not collide Encampment with the required Treasures, it's usually better to play the Encampment than not, as there's a chance you could draw the required cards, and drawing now in order to have a full turn is usually better than passing a turn to hoard an Encampment. Rarely, it can be tactically wise to return Encampments by choosing not to reveal a Gold or Plunder. This might be done to avoid a pileout or slow down your opponent in gaining Plunders.
It is very cheap at . This has two repercussions:
It is easy to pick up with an extra Buy and , or with gainers, especially Workshop variants. Encampment's value vastly exceeds its pricepoint (assuming you can keep it), and as such it makes cards like far more valuable than usual, both because such effects give you more control over the Encampment split and because you're adding stronger-than-usual cards to your deck.
It is an exceptionally strong target for Command variants such as , , , and . In these cases, you get the benefit of playing Encampment but without having to set anything aside or return anything to the supply no matter what, allowing the card playing Encampment to effectively act as a Lost City with no downsides.

[Archetype: Encampment] In games where Encampments are desirable and retained, Plunder is likely to be uncovered. This is usually the case in Kingdoms with sources of gains or +Buy, and decent thinning. Plunder offers a theoretically unbounded but slowly accumulating source of alt-VP, bottlenecked by both the number of copies you possess and how consistently you can play them all. As with all stop card payload, playing all of your Plunders consistently is usually dependent on a strong engine, particularly one with good draw. In Kingdoms that offer a megaturn or a fast pileout, Plunder is often too slow to matter.

[Archetype: Encampment] Because there are only five copies of each card rather than the usual ten, players will end up with an unequal distribution of copies if the pile empties. In Kingdoms where Encampment or Plunder are important, this may make winning the split more of a consideration than it is with other cards. For example, in a Kingdom where draw or village effects are limited and deck control and terminal space are important, it can be advantageous to win the Encampment split. Doing so may allow you to play one more copy of than your opponents, enabling you to gain with . Similarly, winning the split can be important to consider with Plunder, as if your engine can consistently play three Plunders every turn to your opponent's two, you'll outscore them in the long run.

[Strategy Overview: Enchantress] Enchantress is an Action-Attack-Duration card from . It is a turn-worsening attack that turns the first Action each other player plays on their turns into a simple cantrip, although it does not affect effects below dividing lines. It also provides Duration draw. It is the first card to change effects on other players' cards.

[FAQ: Enchantress] =
Players revealing a card like when this is played have to do it right then, not later, even though the attack will not hurt them until their turn.
While this is in play, the first Action each other player plays on each of their turns* will give them +1 Card +1 Action instead of what it would have normally done.
This does not affect abilities below a dividing line; they still function.
For example a player playing would get +1 Card +1 Action and not do anything Sacrifice normally does; a player playing † would get +1 Card +1 Action and would still get for gaining Victory cards.
It can be helpful to turn the affected card sideways, to remember that it did not do what it normally did.
Enchantress does not affect card abilities from cards played on previous turns; for example if an opponent plays Enchantress and you have an out from a previous turn, on your turn you will first get a card from your Archive as normal, and then the first Action card actually played on that turn will be affected by Enchantress.
If Enchantress affects a played in a Buy phase, its player gets +1 Card +1 Action, but has no way to use the +1 Action, since it is their Buy phase (but it might matter e.g. if the player buys ).
If your opponents play multiple Enchantresses in a round, they still only affect the first card you play on each turn, and not any further cards.

=

[FAQ: Enchantress] =
* The rulebook now clarifies that opponents' extra turns will also be Enchanted.
† Many cards, including , got in 2022 and no longer have dividing lines. However, other cards still have dividing line effects (e.g. and ), and those effects will still work, even if Enchanted.
Just like a played during your Buy phase, if the first Action you play during a turn is a at Night, you'll still get +1 Card and +1 Action (and have no way to use the +1 Action).
If a player under the effect of an Enchantress takes one or more extra turns, the first Action played on each of those turns is also turned into a cantrip.
If a Duration card gets affected by Enchantress, it will get discarded from play this turn. This doesn't stop any below-the-line effects.
Enchantress does not change anything about the card, it just prevents on-play instructions on the card from being carried out properly. So cards that are Enchanted can still activate Reactions (such as another player's ), still carry out all Adventures tokens effects (such as the from ), and can still be replayed (e.g. by ) to perform their normal effects.
Enchantress, , and Ways make you ignore an Action's instructions. Because they have the same timing, you can choose which of those effects will win.
However, Way of the Chameleon still makes you follow the Action's instructions, which means Enchantress will stop it (and you'll get +1 Card and +1 Action). 
Unlike Highwayman, this only affects the first Action that a player plays during their turn. So if they play an Action when it isn't their turn (e.g. ), it will be unaffected.

[Strategy Overview: Enchantress] Enchantress provides a small amount of Duration draw and an Attack which can occasionally cause duds or nullify an opponent's key Action card. As Duration draw, it has the usual advantages of not drawing Action cards dead and giving you an increased start-of-turn hand size, generally improving the reliability of your deck; however, it also has the usual disadvantage of Durations, in that it requires you to gain twice as many copies in order to benefit from the effect consistently (because you can play each copy at most every other turn). Additionally, Enchantress is terminal and (as a stop card on the turn you play it) provides a net hand size increase of only one card across each pair of turns. For these reasons, Enchantress is not ideal as a primary source of draw. Since the attack also doesn't stack, it is often best to have just two copies that you play on alternating turns for the attack and reliability benefits. However, additional copies may be necessary if there is no better alternative for draw.

[Strategy Overview: Enchantress] The impact of Enchantress's attack varies greatly depending on two main factors: how much worse your opponent's turn is without the affected Action card, and whether they can play around the nullification. For example, if your opponent only has a single Action card in their hand and you are able to play Enchantress, that Action card will generate almost no benefit for your opponent. This can be the case in the early game, and Enchantress's attack can be high impact if it negates a high-priority Action card — often a trasher such as or an attack such as — though there is a fairly high amount of variance as to whether you can play your Enchantress at the right time for this to occur. Later in the game, when your opponents have more Action cards, Enchantress is more likely to have the effect of slightly reducing your opponents' terminal space (if it hits a village) or draw. It is thus more impactful against fragile decks (such as those relying on playing a particular combination of cards, e.g. a village and a terminal draw card, at the start of each turn), and unlikely to do anything against decks that have many cantrips.

[Strategy Overview: Enchantress] There are a few options to counter Enchantress's attack:
The simplest method is to simply sacrifice an Action card that is not much better than a blank cantrip. This requires both having suitable cards (e.g. ), and having some way to ensure they are in your starting hand. Most often, this will occur as a byproduct of getting deck control and building normally, but you can also use Duration draw (including Enchantress itself) and other similar effects to increase your search space. 
Another light counter to the attack, which may pay off particularly well in the midgame, is to have a deck that is slightly overterminaled, theoretically allowing you to choose which terminals are most beneficial on any given turn and use one that you couldn't play in any case as a sacrifice to the Enchantress attack. 
Since the attack does not negate below-the-line effects of cards, cards that are primarily valued for below-the-line effects (especially early Travellers like ) are good choices to play while under Enchantress attack.
Since Ways override Enchantress's attack, it can sometimes be preferable to play your first card using a Way. is particularly notable, as you can completely negate the attack at the expense of one low-value card, which you can keep setting aside and re-playing with this Way every turn.

[Strategy Overview: Enchantress] Enchantress's value can vary due to interactions with start-of-turn effects. For example, it can helpfully increase your search space for effects such as , , or . However, effects that involve start-of-turn plays of Action cards (e.g. ) can be slightly weakened by the Enchantress attack, as it's not possible to first sacrifice a weak card from your hand to play as a cantrip.

[FAQ: Engineer] =
Engineer cannot gain copies of itself, or any other card with in the cost.
When you play it, you gain a card, then may trash Engineer to gain a second card (which can be the same as the first or different).

[Archetype: Engineer] Like many other Workshop variants, Engineer's relevance in a Kingdom is mostly dependent on the presence of cheap cards that are useful in bulk, such as or . While Engineer's strength and use cases are broadly the same as many other Workshop variants, its one-shot effect and cost have some important consequences.

[Archetype: Engineer] Engineer's one-shot effect to gain two cards offers it extra reach and flexibility. Often, this effect is useful to apply pile pressure when piles are low, either for a pileout or to win the split of a key card. Outside of such cases, you should still consider whether trashing an Engineer is advantageous. Oftentimes this will be once its nature as a terminal stop card outweighs the benefit of gaining extra cheap cards, be it because a key pile of gainable cards has emptied, or because terminal space is scarce and some other terminal offers more benefit.

[Opening: Engineer] The cost of Engineer has a few implications. First, Engineer cannot gain additional copies of itself, nor can it be gained by most other gainers. In Kingdoms where using multiple Engineers is worthwhile, you will typically have to spend your Buys on the Engineers, and use the Engineers themselves to gain support pieces (e.g. villages). Additionally, like other cards, Engineer's cost allows you to put more value into a reshuffle than you could otherwise, and so buying one at the end of a shuffle (e.g. turn 2) is often the best time to do so. For example, on a in the opening, you wouldn't be able to open with both and , but can open with Recruiter and Engineer. This can sometimes be useful when combined with Engineer's one-shot effect, as you can effectively convert the cost of another cheap card into . For example, if you would only have and a Buy towards another , you can trash your Engineer, gaining the Caravan, and buy a replacement Engineer.
[Archetype: Engineer] Like other Workshop variants, Engineer synergizes well with cost reduction effects such as that of , which allow it to gain more expensive or powerful cards.

[FAQ: Farmers' Market] =
If you gain this from , or when it's set aside with , there is no Farmers' Market pile. This means no tokens will accumulate, and you won't get any ; the card does nothing but +1 Buy.
If you play this with a Command variant, the Farmers' Market will fail to trash itself, but you still take .

[Opening: Farmers' Market] Farmers' Market is a terminal payload card that generates varying amounts of alongside +Buy, eventually turning into a one-shot source of . In terms of generation, Farmers' Market is on average a terminal Silver, which on its own is a weak effect. It will almost never be worth opening with, as its first play gives , which is very poor at hitting key price points. Farmer's Market may be important if you need the +Buy effect. However, as a terminal stop card whose offering beyond the +Buy is fairly weak, it will often be outclassed by other +Buy sources in the Kingdom. The bonus on every fifth play can be helpful but will usually not be a sufficient reason to gain Farmer's Market nor a sufficient source of on its own, as it provides even less than at an average of 0.8 points per play, and taking the means you will need a replacement for your source of +Buy.

[Strategy Overview: Farmers' Market] Since the five-play cycle governing Farmers' Market's effect applies to the plays of all players and the higher plays later in the cycle provide much more value, controlling which plays of the cycle you get can be important. In the early game when you're only able to play Farmers' Market every couple of turns, it has a significant first mover disadvantage, as every play is very likely to just give your opponent more on their next play, and so it's generally not worth gaining early. Later in the game, as the value of rises and you are able to play multiple Farmers' Markets in a turn, it is best to avoid giving your opponent the higher value plays with two or more tokens on the pile, and instead finish a cycle and take the if you can. Effective ways to manipulate the token count to your advantage include: staggering your gains of Farmers' Market based on the current stack and how many copies you expect your opponent to play; or manipulating the number of Farmers' Markets you play on your turn using Throne Room variants or Command cards; or simply not playing excess Farmers' Markets.

[Strategy Overview: Farmers' Market] Command cards and similar effects have an additional synergy with Farmers' Market, as the is not conditional on the card being successfully trashed. For example, if you only ever play Farmers' Market via , you can continually generate as long as the pile isn't emptied by your opponent. In situations where you do have a mix of Farmers' Markets and Command cards that you want to use to play Farmers' Market, it is best to order them so that you play a Command card when the stack has so that you can claim the without penalty.

[Strategy Overview: Forum] Forum is an Action card from . It is a sifter that maintains hand size.

[FAQ: Forum] =
For example, with and only one Buy, you could buy a Forum, getting +1 Buy, then buy a .

[Archetype: Forum] Forum is a handsize-neutral and non-terminal sifter that offers effective cycling through your deck towards the cards you most want to find. This means it offers a strong boost to your deck's reliability, and makes it a very useful support card. However, at , it often has a significant opportunity cost, and so it's common to want to add it after higher priority cards such as a trasher. Forum is particularly useful in Kingdoms in which other deck control tools (in the form of thinning and perhaps draw) are weak or lacking, or in the early midgame before you are able to attain deck control. The increased reliability it offers is particularly relevant if you need to find the right combination of cards each turn to get your engine going, such as a village and terminal draw, or and . Otherwise, it can also be a very strong tool for facilitating other specific card collisions (for example with ).

[Archetype: Forum] Although it's relatively expensive, Forum's on-gain effect means that it has no opportunity cost in terms of gains. This has several implications: 
It's often worth purchasing on a turn on which you've generated more than you can make use of with the number of Buys available. For example, if you have and 1 Buy and need a , buying Forum first effectively allows you to acquire it for free.
Forums can be very easy to empty for a pileout or acquire in bulk: in particular, if you have significant cost reduction (e.g., via ), this can be achieved cheaply and at net cost of zero Buys. 
Using a gainer such as to gain Forum functions as a one-time source of +Buy; this may be important if +Buy is otherwise difficult to come by, but the fact that it is not sustainable once the Forum pile runs out means that it is most commonly useful in the midgame as a way to pick up extra engine pieces. This can potentially also be used to buy multiple Victory cards per turn during greening, although it may be risky to plan around Forums remaining available in the pile late into the game.

[Strategy Overview: Forum] Forum becomes significantly more powerful if you can also benefit from synergy with Reactions that trigger on discard: notably, Forum paired with or can function as draw in addition to providing a village effect.
[Strategy Overview: Fountain] Fountain is a Landmark from . It gives a large amount of to players who end the game with at least 10 in their deck.

[FAQ: Fountain] =
You either get or ; there is no extra bonus for having 20 .

[Archetype: Fountain] Fountain offers a tradeoff of a large amount of for a large amount of stop cards. While is 2.5 times as much as a , Fountain is far less efficient in terms of per stop card, at per card. Most engines will be unable to draw the deck with ten additional stop cards, and would much prefer to be using other, more efficient payload anyway.

[Archetype: Fountain] Fountain's relevance in a Kingdom depends on three factors:
How much is worth relative to other scoring options. If a megaturn gaining all eight Provinces is available with , in exchange for carrying around ten stop cards is very much not worth it. If the Kingdom only offers a money strategy of moderate speed that can only score a Province a turn, Fountain is much more relevant.
How bad is for your deck, and what your options for getting rid of it are. If there's good payload, Copper is inefficient by comparison. If the draw is somewhat unreliable or dependent on collisions (e.g., / engines), Coppers are junk getting in the way of that. A strong trasher such as can solve both of these problems and enable a strong engine that will make Fountain less relevant. If, however, there is no Copper trashing, the first three Coppers bought are effectively worth each, which may be worth buying if you miss Province in the greening stage, even in single-buy Kingdoms.
The availability of additional gains, particularly +Buy and Copper gainers such as and . Without these options Fountain is much less appealing as the high opportunity cost of spending your one Buy per turn on a Copper comes into play, and in many cases spending three turns buying a Copper is both less efficient scoring than buying Province and does not improve the quality of your deck. In games with plentiful +Buy, it's often viable to trash your starting Coppers with the plan to rebuy them in the endgame for scoring with Fountain.

[Strategy Overview: Fountain] Fountain synergizes with effects that can keep your starting Coppers around for scoring purposes but outside of your drawable deck. Exilers such as are strong for this purpose, both thinning your deck in the early game and allowing you to more easily pursue Fountain later. Effects such as those of and can also be suitable for this purpose.

[Strategy Overview: Groundskeeper] =
This can trigger multiple times in a turn, for cards gained different ways.
For example you could play Groundskeeper, then play gaining an and taking , then in your Buy phase buy a taking another .
Multiple Groundskeepers are cumulative.
