[Strategy Overview: Outpost] Outpost is an Action-Duration card from . When played, it lets you take an extra turn — albeit an extra turn where you draw fewer cards.

[FAQ: Outpost] =
The extra turn is normal except for the smaller starting hand.
If for example you play and Outpost on the same turn, then the extra turn will be your next turn, and you'll get from Merchant Ship then.
Outpost always causes you to only draw 3 cards for your next hand, but only gives you an extra turn if, when the time comes for that turn, you have not taken the previous two turns.
So if you play two Outposts on the same turn, you will only get one extra turn; if you play an Outpost during an extra turn, it will give you a smaller hand but won't give you an extra turn.
Extra turns do not count towards the tiebreaker of which tied player had fewer turns.

=

[FAQ: Outpost] =
You don't get to take any extra turns after the game has ended.
With , you can take extra turns after the normal game end, but not after the final Fleet turn.
You only draw 3 cards, even if you know you won't get the extra turn, and even if the Outpost turn won't be the next turn.
If you play Outpost multiple times with a card like Throne Room, stays in play with Outpost even though you won't get a second extra turn.
You can't be sure Outpost will fail until you are in between turns, determining what turn happens next. If Outpost fails, or its turn is skipped by , you discard it during Clean-up of the next turn, whoever's turn it is.
For example, if you play Outpost and , then in Clean-up you don't discard either of them and only draw 3 cards. In between turns, you choose to take the Voyage turn next. (Outpost hasn't failed yet because another player might somehow be able to take an extra turn first.) In Clean-up of the Voyage turn, you discard Voyage but not Outpost and draw 5 cards. In between turns again, Outpost now fails - it is up next but would be your 3rd turn in a row. The next player takes their turn and during their Clean-up, you discard Outpost.
If you play Outpost on another player's turn, you draw 3 cards in your next Clean-up, which is usually during the Outpost turn.
If multiple players play Outpost on the same turn, the extra turns go in turn order, starting with the player who took the most recent regular turn.
You can take 2 Outpost turns in a row if the preceding turn wasn't yours.

[Archetype: Outpost] Outpost is generally a very powerful card when the Kingdom offers an engine that's strong enough to allow you to play it consistently and achieve as much on each extra turn as you can on your main turns; this is comparable to doubling your deck's payload (including the 1 Buy you start all turns with). However, if your deck is such that the three-card starting hand makes your Outpost turns mostly very weak, it's unlikely to be worthwhile unless you can put these turns to some specific use in improving your next main turn.

[Archetype: Outpost] Outpost is highly valuable when you've thinned your deck thoroughly and built a reliable engine so that you stand a good chance of drawing your deck even when starting from a smaller hand. Gaining Outpost before achieving deck control, or in a Kingdom that doesn't offer it at all, is less likely to pay off. Outpost is better still if there's a way to further increase your chances of a full Outpost turn, such as duration draw (e.g. ), topdecking of draw cards (e.g. with ), or draw-to-x (e.g. ). is also a reliable way to draw a new five-card hand at the start of each Outpost turn.

[Strategy Overview: Outpost] Once all players are using Outpost consistently and effectively, the net outcome is to change how the turns are ordered, with everyone getting two turns at a time. This has a strong impact on endgame considerations: when deciding when to begin greening, how much you need to score on a particular turn, or whether it's safe to lower the Supply piles, you must account for the fact that more turns than usual will take place before your next one. Since you can also use two turns to close out the game, Outpost can occasionally produce risky endgame plays in which (for example) you lower the piles significantly on the assumption that you'll be able to win on your Outpost turn, but you leave your opponent an easy victory if that turn is a dud.

[Archetype: Outpost] The asymmetry between a player using Outpost effectively and one who's not using it is unusually great, especially since only one copy of the card is needed to access its full power. This means that a slow engine is somewhat more likely to be worth building in the presence of Outpost, and it can be a game-winning purchase from the .

[Strategy Overview: Outpost] Outpost's interaction with Duration cards brings certain trade-offs. In addition to the boost provided by Duration draw, you also need only half as many copies as usual of any Duration card that you want to keep in play during your opponents' turns, e.g. or , as you can play them on your Outpost turn and discard them from play at the end of your main turn. However, this has to be managed carefully and means any start-of-turn benefit can only be reaped on your main turns.
[Strategy Overview: Outpost] It is also worth noting that if you play a †, your opponent will not get an additional turn by playing Outpost on their next turn.

[Strategy Overview: Pearl Diver] is an Action card from the first edition of . It is a cantrip, and thus it rarely hurts to have it in your deck, but its ability to move cards from the bottom of your deck to the top rarely helps very much either.
[Strategy Overview: Pearl Diver] It was removed from the second edition of Seaside and replaced with , which is a more useful cantrip that can sometimes act as non-terminal draw.

[FAQ: Pearl Diver] =
First draw a card and get +1 Action; then look at the bottom card of your deck, shuffling first if needed.
If you choose to put the bottom card on top of your deck, be sure not to look at the card above it.

[Strategy Overview: Pearl Diver] Before it was removed, Pearl Diver was a cheap but weak cantrip. While it does not usually hurt your deck, it doesn’t provide much value either so you will mostly gain it when you cannot buy more expensive cards and there are no alternatives available.

[Strategy Overview: Pearl Diver] Pearl Diver is most commonly useful for various purposes where cheap cantrips are generally applicable:
As a means to increase the density of Action cards in your deck without affecting your deck control. This is useful when scoring with effects such as , or for drawing with effects such as or .
As cheap targets for on-buy effects such as those of and , or to facilitate a three-pile ending. 
As a cheap non-terminal target for Throne Room variants and Adventures tokens (such as from ). 
As a cheap way to access powerful Ways such as .

[Opening: Pearl Diver] The option to inspect and topdeck the bottom card of your deck is generally low impact, as even at its best it will never offer an improvement over simply having better shuffle luck. Pearl Diver may occasionally allow you to make sure an important card does not miss the shuffle, but in the early stages of the game where this is important, Pearl Diver has some opportunity cost and the most likely outcome is that you'll simply look at a junk card. Pearl Diver is an option on a opening split because it nearly guarantees that the other card bought in the opening does not miss the reshuffle. However, given the low impact of the bottomdeck inspection, many other cheap cantrips (e.g., ) are overall preferable for the use cases outlined above because they offer a better additional effect.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate] It replaces the removed first-edition card , a less interesting Treasure gainer.

[FAQ: Pirate] =
You can play this when you gain a Treasure, or when another player gains a Treasure.
If you play this during another player's turn, your following turn is when your Pirate gains you a Treasure.
The Treasure you gain comes from the Supply and goes directly to your hand.

=

[FAQ: Pirate] =
This plays like the Reactions in ; see the Reactions section.
If you have , gaining an Action with in its text will let all players play Pirates from their hand.

[Archetype: Pirate] Pirate is a relatively slow gainer and payload card that can help fill your deck with and other high-value Treasures. Because it is a terminal stop card that provides no value on the turn you play it, and Treasures are usually not the most efficient way to scale your payload, Pirate is usually more valuable in weaker Kingdoms. If alternatives for additional gains are scarce, free may become valuable, particularly in money strategies. Alternatively, Pirate can be a useful gainer for stronger engines if powerful Kingdom Treasures (such as or are available.
 
Although Pirate is terminal, its Reaction allows it to be played non-terminally when a Treasure is gained. Additionally, reacting with a Pirate during an opponent's turn has the added advantage of letting your Pirate take effect a turn sooner. For these reasons, Pirate is much more effective when played via its Reaction, and it's worth evaluating how often you will be able to do so. There are a few main considerations with respect to Pirate's Reaction:
Pirate self-synergizes, as a Pirate in hand can react to the start-of-turn Treasure gain from a previously played Pirate (either yours or an opponent's). This can make Pirate more appealing if you or an opponent already have some in deck.
If you have a Pirate in hand that you lack the Actions to play (e.g. if you drew it dead with ), you can buy a Treasure card to play it. This is particularly relevant in money strategies, which generally want to limit the number of terminal Action cards they play with to avoid terminal collision. Because Pirate can easily be made non-terminal, it can be paired with other terminal Actions such as terminal draw cards with less risk of becoming overterminaled.
Reacting with a Pirate during an opponent's turn is important not only because it speeds up Pirate, but also because it gives you more control over your generation. For example, if you draw a Pirate during clean-up of turn 9 and then react with it to an opponent's Treasure gain on their turn, you can gain a at the start of turn 10, whereas without the Reaction, you would need to play the Pirate on your turn 10 to gain a turn 11. In rare circumstances, however, you may want to decline triggering Pirate on the opponent's turn and instead play it on your own, most likely because it would be better to gain a on the subsequent turn since your hand already has enough to afford a .
When playing against an opponent who has Pirates, you may want to minimize gaining Treasures, especially in cases where they are likely to have a Pirate in hand (which you can determine via deck tracking). For example, buying a late game with a leftover might be worse than nothing if it gives the opponent the immediate benefit of expediting their Pirate plays.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate] The fact that Pirate gains to hand gives it some small advantages. Most notably, Pirate can provide more immediate payload than many other Treasure gainers, as you only need to wait one turn rather than a full reshuffle. This effect is more pronounced in money strategies, where you typically don't cycle and reshuffle very often. Otherwise, Pirate adding a Treasure onto a 5-card hand can allow you to spike a price point, or provide some reliability when your draw is reliant on .

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] Although Pirate Ship often gets a reputation among inexperienced players as a powerful card, it is actually very rarely effective, and using it often benefits your opponents more than you.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] It was removed from the second edition of Seaside and replaced with , which is also a Treasure-trashing Attack but is a stronger card, most notably because it cannot relieve the attacked player of unwanted .

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] Before it was removed, Pirate Ship was an exceedingly weak card, whose Attack was often beneficial to your opponents and whose payload increased in strength far too slowly for most Kingdoms.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] The fundamental paradox of Pirate Ship is that in order for its payload to reach a useful amount, you need to play it multiple times, which usually entails playing it early in the game; but if you play it early in the game, you're very likely to cycle through your opponents' bad starting cards and trash their for them, speeding up their progress and removing junk from their decks. Because of how much it helps your opponents, Pirate Ship is very weak in the early game, and it usually takes too long to generate significant if you don't start playing it until the late game. Even disregarding the help it gives your opponents, having a terminal card give you no benefit this turn for on every subsequent play (assuming it hits an opponent's Treasure) is not a strong effect in the early game, when you typically want to establish deck control rather than begin accumulating payload. Additionally, Pirate Ship's eventual payoff to opportunity cost ratio is low, as even after two plays it's at best a terminal silver, or after three plays a terminal , which compares unfavorably to just buying those Treasures instead of Pirate Ship.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] The other main weakness of Pirate Ship is that Treasures are very often not the best payload cards on the board, and so a strong deck can often be built of mostly Action cards. This means that Pirate Ship is unlikely either to find a Treasure to trash in the later stages of the game (thus failing to increase its generation) or to be of any threat to your opponents' payload.

[Strategy Overview: Pirate Ship] Pirate Ship can be usable in fairly rare scenarios in which multiple of the above generalizations are not true. When Treasures are the only payload, Pirate Ship may actually have a chance to harm your opponents' economy. Additionally, with a Throne Room variant such as , it may be possible to play Pirate Ship enough times in the late game for it to generate significant . Similarly, makes it easier to consistently play Pirate Ship many times, while also reducing the amount of terminal space normally needed to do so and providing the flexibility to be played as something other than a terminal stop card, Kingdom permitting. Pirate Ship's strongest synergy is with , which changes almost all payload cards into Treasures. As such, it greatly increases Pirate Ship's effectiveness as an Attack while also allowing you to play it in the Buy phase, which removes the need for village support.

[Opening: Salvager] Salvager is a trash for benefit card that also provides +Buy. Salvager often sees use as a thinner in the early game. Because it generates while doing so, opening with Salvager can allow you to still hit early price points while removing the worst cards in your starting deck. However, as a terminal that provides very little benefit while trashing , it is generally less useful if you fail to collide it with your Estates, and is much less useful after you run out of Estates. As such, you will usually not get much value out of additional copies, and Salvager is likely skippable if stronger trashers that can deal with Coppers (e.g. ) are available, or if you need to prioritize the terminal space for something else (e.g. advancing your ).

[Archetype: Salvager] While the extra Buy can sometimes be important in the early game to gain cheap engine pieces, it will generally increase in relevance later as you start to have better generation. In the endgame, the extra Buy offers extra flexibility especially with the potential spike in from trashing something. Notably, Salvager can mill ; trashing a Province provides both the and the Buy necessary to get a new copy.

[Strategy Overview: Salvager] Like many other trash-for-benefit cards, Salvager synergizes with effects that can provide you with expensive fodder. If the source can do so consistently (e.g. or a gainer like ), Salvager may be able to generate money and Buys sustainably. Other sources may be less sustainable, but still useful. This may include cards with on-trash effects (e.g. ), you bought cheaply, cards with on-gain effects that provide minimal benefit after gaining (for example, ), or Actions you no longer need (such as a curser after the pile empties).

[Strategy Overview: Sea Chart] is an Action card from the second edition of . It's a cantrip that provides an additional card of non-terminal draw if the additional card is a copy of one you already have in play. It is intended to combine especially well with Duration cards you have multiple copies of, since you may have one already be in play from the previous turn when you play Sea Chart.

[Strategy Overview: Sea Hag] Sea Hag is an Action-Attack card from the first edition of . It is a curser which puts cards on top of an opponent's deck.

[Strategy Overview: Sea Hag] It was removed from the second edition of Seaside and replaced with , which is also a curser but ties into the set's Duration theme and offers benefits besides the attack.

They go onto decks rather than into discard piles.

=

[Strategy Overview: Sea Hag] Before it was removed, Sea Hag was a junking attack of varying impact. Unlike other cursers, Sea Hag offers no benefit beyond the attack, and therefore its strength rests entirely on how important that attack is in a particular Kingdom. your opponents is generally a powerful effect (especially early in the game), so Sea Hag can be an essential card; however, it's unlikely to be useful if the Kingdom offers reasonably strong trashing or a better junking attack, such as . When the Kingdom encourages you towards Sea Hag, gaining one early is usually a good plan.

[Archetype: Sea Hag] Sea Hag's main downside is that it takes up terminal space while doing nothing to help you either control your own deck or increase its payload; this also means it eventually becomes useless after the Curses run out. Compared to cards like Witch, it's also relatively slow to distribute Curses, although their impact occurs sooner than usual since they're deposited onto the top of your opponents' decks. It's possible to cause serious damage in this way: early in the game, drawing the Curse instead of a may hurt an opponent's purchasing power substantially, and later on there are situations in which it might be enough to prevent them from drawing their deck (e.g. if they have a weak or unreliable engine). However, occasionally the topdecking simply makes it easier for an opponent to deal with the Curse before it can contaminate their next shuffle, for instance if their trasher is , which benefits from the certainty that its target is on top of the deck. There are a few situations (e.g. with ) in which the ability to manipulate the top card of an opponent's deck could be useful for other reasons.

[Strategy Overview: Sea Hag] Beyond its use in Kingdoms which lack Curse-trashing, Sea Hag might occasionally pay off in a situation in which you believe you're ahead of your opponents, and therefore you can afford the opportunity costs of Sea Hag more readily than they can deal with the incoming Curses; similar considerations may, rarely, lead you to gain a second Sea Hag in the midgame. The availability of a trash-for-benefit effect to eventually get rid of Sea Hag can also enhance its attractiveness.

[FAQ: Smugglers] =
First you choose a card that the player to your right gained costing up to , and then you (try to) gain a copy of it from the Supply. This means you may be able to choose a card that is unavailable to be gained (e.g., a card whose Supply pile is empty, or a card from outside the Supply); and if you do, you gain nothing.
It doesn't matter where the gained card came from. So if the player to your right gained a card from the trash with , you can gain a copy of that card (from the Supply).
If your opponent gained a Ruins or a , Smugglers will only let you gain a copy if the top card of the Ruins or Knights pile has the same name.
Cards with or in their cost can never be gained with Smugglers.
If you play this during the player to your right's turn (e.g. they play , trash your , and you gain a that lets you play Smugglers), this checks that player's last completed turn, not their current turn.

[Opening: Smugglers] Smugglers is a gainer for cards that cost up to , but can only target cards gained by your opponent during their previous turn. Its value therefore depends on the availability of alternative sources of extra gains and the likelihood of gaining a useful card, as well as your ability to reliably play Smugglers. If it is easy to play a large number of Smugglers, this can somewhat disincentivize the gaining of useful cards that would activate an opponent's Smugglers; in an extreme case, it can be preferable to gain nothing until you can afford a card out of the price range of Smugglers. 
 
Smugglers is a terminal stop card, so it ideally needs the support of both draw and sufficient terminal space. Since it is also completely useless unless your opponent has gained a desirable and eligible card on their previous turn, it works best if they will regularly do so and if you have sufficient deck control to play Smugglers at the right time—preferably every turn, and ideally with overdraw that allows you to use it for gain-and-play. If your opponent only occasionally gains cards useful to you and you only occasionally play Smugglers, there is a strong risk that those turns will not coincide, heavily reducing the benefit you can get out of Smugglers. For this reason, while the potential payoff can be high, Smugglers is unreliable in the opening and early game, although certain effects like or may enable you to have Smugglers available at just the right time.

[Strategy Overview: Smugglers] Smugglers may be skippable if there are other gainers available: notably, most Workshop variants are usually preferable for gaining cards that cost up to because they are independent of your opponent's choices and often offer some additional benefit. If such alternatives are lacking, Smugglers may be useful to gain cheap cards that you would otherwise use these alternatives for. Additionally, since Smugglers can also gain cards costing or , it is often especially relevant in contesting piles of key cards at these prices, like ; is a particularly notable example because the difficulty of gaining it goes beyond its price. In the endgame, the same considerations apply to gaining for extra . In some cases it is important to weigh up the advantage of buying a Duchy for yourself against the risk of your opponent gaining multiple copies using Smugglers. 
 
A few other aspects of the Kingdom can also modulate the value of Smugglers. Given that the gaining effect has an upper cost limit, Smugglers synergizes with cost reduction. Most notably, with costs reduced by it can gain a whenever your opponent gains one. On the other hand, Smugglers may be significantly devalued in the presence of key cards it is unable to gain, including those costing , like , or , like . Similar considerations apply with cards that are only available as a single copy, notably from the but also in the case of and , or that are not in the Supply (e.g. ).

[Strategy Overview: Tactician] Tactician is an Action-Duration card from . Tactician allows you to discard your hand this turn in order to essentially double your next turn: you get five extra cards in addition to one extra buy and one extra action.

[FAQ: Tactician] =
If you have no cards in hand, then Tactician does nothing more and is discarded in the same turn's Clean-up.
If you do have at least one card, you discard your hand, Tactician stays in play, and at the start of your next turn you get +5 Cards, +1 Buy, and +1 Action (and Tactician is discarded that turn).
If you use on Tactician, you will discard your hand on the first play and will have no cards in hand for the second play (and so will not get the bonuses).

=

[FAQ: Tactician] =
You can Throne Room a Tactician, but (under ordinary circumstances) you do not get any extra cards (as described above). Still the Throne Room (or its variants) stays in play.
Like all Duration cards, Tactician only stays in play during your Clean-up phase if it will do something in a future turn. So, if you play Tactician but do not discard any cards, it will have no effect on your next turn and should be discarded during the same turn's Clean-up phase.
When the is on Tactician, using a Throne Room variant on it becomes meaningful as it provides you a card to discard each time Tactician is played again.

[Strategy Overview: Tactician] Tactician is generally used in one of two ways: Either you play Tactician at most every other turn, sacrificing your ability to use Treasures to buy cards on some turns in order to have other turns in which you can accomplish more with your deck; or you try to play one every turn, relying entirely on Action-based payload. These strategies are colloquially called Single Tactician and Double Tactician, respectively. Despite being the less obvious strategy, it is Double Tactician which reveals the real strength of the card: in Kingdoms with Tactician, you should think carefully at the start of the game about whether a Double Tactician strategy is viable.

[Strategy Overview: Tactician] Because playing Tactician is costly, you need a reason to build a Double Tactician deck. If draw is plentiful and reliable, and +Buy is easy to come by, skipping Tactician is likely to be the right choice. But if Tactician is the only source of +Buy or draw is unreliable, a Double Tactician deck is likely to be a strong option — if one can be built. A Double Tactician deck also typically needs Action-based sources of (maybe ) and a way to thin or sift through enough of your starting cards to allow you to reliably draw your Action payload and another Tactician. Note that Tactician is not generally a village in Double Tactician decks: you start each turn with two Actions, but you need to use one of those to play Tactician, so without another source of +Actions you can still only play one other terminal card.

[Strategy Overview: Tactician] Tactician synergizes with a number of cards which reduce the downside of having to discard your hand. The strongest synergies are with cards that let you play Tactician after you play your Treasures, such as in the combo with . functions similarly. Tactician also synergizes with and , which turn your excess hand size into . Especially when using these cards, it is important to remember that Tactician needs to discard at least one card to activate its effect, so you should make sure to keep a card in hand for this purpose.

[Archetype: Tactician] Single Tactician tends to be viable in an entirely different type of Kingdom. The main use case is in a slog, where it is hard to do much on a typical turn because your deck is filled with junk. Here, Tactician can help you to gain the occasional so that you can score more than your opponent. Tactician can also help you to line up cards that work together, such as and Province.

[Strategy Overview: Tactician] Tactician can also be purchased for a specific short-term purpose: You might buy one early to spike and purchase or to trigger , or you might purchase one later on with to set up your next turn. Although it's rarely viable, players can sometimes aim to play more than one Tactician per turn: For example, if you a Tactician in a hand with , you can play the discarded Village Green to draw another card, allowing your second Tactician play to take effect.

[Strategy Overview: Treasure Map] Treasure Map is an Action card from . By itself, a single Treasure Map does nothing besides trash itself; however, when you play a Treasure Map and have a second one in your hand, you gain 4 cards and put them on your deck.

[FAQ: Treasure Map] =
When you play this, you trash it and trash another Treasure Map from your hand, if you can.
If you did trash another copy of Treasure Map, then you gain 4 , putting them onto your deck instead of your discard pile.
If you did not have another copy of Treasure Map in hand, then you just trash the played Treasure Map and nothing else happens.
Using on Treasure Map is not trashing two Treasure Maps.

[FAQ: Treasure Map] =
If you play a Command variant such as , which then plays a Treasure Map, you can't trash the Treasure Map from the Supply, so you will fail to gain 4 Golds.
If you someone and make them trash 2 Treasure Maps, both of them are set aside, and you gain 4 Golds to your discard pile.
Both Treasure Maps have to be trashed at once. This means that if you draw the second Treasure Map as a result of trashing the first one (e.g. by trashing a to ), you can't trash the second Treasure Map for 4 Golds.

[Strategy Overview: Treasure Map] Treasure Map is not usually a very useful card. First, its -gaining ability is inconvenient to activate: it requires you to spend two gains on Treasure Maps, then collide the two copies in your hand and use some of your terminal space to play one of them. Second, its effect when activated is generally not helpful. In many Kingdoms, you'll be better off generating primarily using Action cards rather than Treasures; even if you do want some Golds in your deck, four of them on top of your drawpile is typically not ideal. Unless you can draw them immediately, you'll start your next turn with four stop cards in your hand, making it unlikely that you'll manage to draw your deck or put the Golds to good use by finding your +Buy, trash-for-benefit cards, or anything else you'd like to collide with them.

[Archetype: Treasure Map] The best-case scenario for making use of Treasure Map is one in which your engine has substantial overdraw, but the Kingdom lacks sufficient Action-based payload and you need to ramp up your production more quickly than by gaining Treasures one by one. In this situation, the ideal way to use Treasure Map is to gain-and-play two of them after drawing your deck, then draw the four Golds as well. You can then make use of them immediately (for or otherwise, e.g. by Remodelling them), while remaining confident that you can draw your deck again next turn.

[Archetype: Treasure Map] Outside this relatively narrow type of situation, e.g. in a money strategy or with an engine that isn't yet reliably overdrawing, gaining two or more Treasure Maps and hoping to draw them together is unlikely to work out well. In Kingdoms that have little else to do, however, strong trashing such as or can enable quick Treasure Map activation and an early start to greening with a deck mostly composed of Golds. In the absence of such trashing, Sifters and effects such as and can also facilitate early collision and may be moderately effective methods of adding Golds.

[FAQ: Treasury] =
This only cares about Victory cards gained in the Buy phase, not ones gained in the Action phase (such as via ).
If you didn't gain any Victory cards in your Buy phase, then when you discard Treasury from play, you can put it on top of your deck (before drawing in Clean-up).
If you gained a Victory card in your Buy phase, all Treasuries are discarded normally in Clean-up.

[Strategy Overview: Treasury] Treasury is a weak and expensive Peddler variant that offers a small amount of consistent payload even if you can't draw your deck, so long as you don't gain Victory cards in your Buy phase. This benefit is usually insufficient to compensate for its significant opportunity cost, in large part because Treasury's topdecking effect is mostly irrelevant when you have good deck control. Conversely, Kingdoms in which deck control is difficult or impossible may lead to earlier greening, negating the bonus. However, Treasury can be a reasonable form of supplementary payload in the absence of better options, especially if draw is weak, as it can be added to your deck in arbitrary amounts without impeding deck control. This is more likely to be viable in games where its opportunity cost is reduced, usually because there is a suitable gainer like or and a shortage of better targets for it; a specific need for Action-based payload (such as with ) can also somewhat increase Treasury's appeal.

[Strategy Overview: Treasury] In occasional cases, the topdecking effect can be relevant even if you have good deck control: for example, it can enhance the benefit of added tokens, particularly the +Card token from . Notably, it can also guarantee that you will have a reasonable target in hand for certain start-of-turn and similar effects, such as or . Treasury’s bonus is somewhat counteracted by discard attacks (particularly ), so this effect has even less value than usual in their presence. Similarly, its value is attenuated if you want to buy Victory cards early, for example to use them with or .
[Strategy Overview: Warehouse] is an Action card from . It is a sifter; when played, it gives +3 cards before you must discard 3 cards.

[FAQ: Warehouse] =
If there are fewer than 3 cards for you to draw (after shuffling), you still discard 3 cards.

[Strategy Overview: Warehouse] Warehouse is a non-terminal sifter which lets you draw then discard cards, but reduces your hand size by one. Warehouse offers a degree of control over which cards you have in hand and rapid cycling, both of which are especially relevant if you lack full deck control, including in the early game. Specifically, the benefits are:
You get to play your strong, newly added cards faster. 
You get more plays of key cards (for example, attacks like , trashers like , or Travellers|).
You have better odds of colliding the right cards to further your gameplan, for example and . 
These benefits becomes less important when it is easy to achieve deck control quickly (for example, with or ).

[Strategy Overview: Warehouse] Once you have achieved deck control, Warehouse can maintain relevance if you rely on finding specific cards or combinations of cards to have a good turn. This might be the case, for example, in a deck relying on villages and terminal draw. A couple copies of Warehouse can be very good for your reliability in such decks.

[Strategy Overview: Warehouse] In some cases, when achieving complete deck control via thinning is difficult or impossible, Warehouse can be a viable alternative, allowing you to find your useful cards and draw past junk. When using Warehouse for this purpose, it is important to avoid triggering an unfavorable reshuffle with just the discarded junk, which could result in one or more dud turns. Using Warehouse this way has two main disadvantages compared to just removing all the junk from your deck. First, your deck is far less reliable; second, it makes it difficult to gain and play cards, which usually end up in your discard pile along with your discarded junk. Instead of simple overdraw you would need to have an effect that topdecks the gained cards (such as ), or that gets them out of your discard pile selectively (such as ).

[Archetype: Warehouse] The hand size reduction aspect of Warehouse may alter its value in the following ways:
If you don't have particularly good cards to find or if most of your cards are of similar quality (e.g., all ), the reduced number of cards may have a larger negative effect than the positive effect from having a better selection.
Warehouse becomes worse in multiples, i.e. while extra copies give you a larger search space, each one also increases the chance that your hand will become too small to do anything useful.
The negative impact of the hand size reduction is exacerbated if you already suffer from a reduced hand size due to a discard attack. 
In draw-to-X engines, Warehouse's hand size reduction matters far less, as you will draw back up to a constant hand size anyway. In fact, Warehouse's ability to discard significant numbers of cards that you cannot play during your Action phase (generally Treasures and Victory cards) often enables you to draw more effectively, as your hand size can now be spent on Action-based payload before playing your draw-to-X card.

[Strategy Overview: Warehouse] Warehouse synergizes with cards that benefit from being discarded: if is a good option for payload, then you can quickly add some to your deck with Warehouse and . Moreover, using Warehouse with generates extra terminal space and is hand size neutral.

[Strategy Overview: Wharf] Wharf is an Action-Duration card from . It is a terminal draw card on this turn, but its duration ability is what makes it one of the best cards in Dominion — drawing 2 cards at the start of your next turn is like having played two .

[FAQ: Wharf] =
You draw 2 cards and get +1 Buy when you play this, and draw another 2 Cards and get +1 Buy at the start of your next turn.

[Strategy Overview: Wharf] Wharf is one of the strongest draw cards in the game. Duration draw is a very powerful effect, since increasing the size of your starting hand next turn raises your chances of colliding the cards you need in order to have a good turn; it's especially useful when your draw cards are terminal and therefore need a village to be played first. As Wharf provides a small amount of draw for your current turn too, it compares very favourably to other Duration draw cards, which are often terminal stop cards when played. The further addition of two extra buys for each Wharf play serves to cement its status as a highly valuable card in most Kingdoms.

[Archetype: Wharf] Some form of deck-drawing engine, often a strong one, is very likely to be possible in Kingdoms with Wharf, as a source of +Actions (or some supplementary non-terminal draw) is the only additional component needed. If this is unavailable, the best approach is likely to be a money strategy with two or three Wharves as your terminals, played on alternating turns. Otherwise, you'll be building an engine in which Wharf's powerful draw means that prioritising early trashing may be slightly less important than it typically is.

[Strategy Overview: Wharf] Obtaining a Wharf quickly is often important, and its presence may be a good reason to prioritise generating in the early game. Getting a Wharf into play increases your deck's early purchasing power and cycling substantially, while providing +Buy to ensure you can put the extra generated to good use; gaining multiple Wharves in relatively quick succession is also not unusual. Although some villages will be needed, each Wharf only takes up a terminal slot every other turn. Even accounting for this, maintaining sufficient terminal space is less critical than with most terminals: although you would prefer not to draw other Action cards dead with Wharf, risking this by playing it as your final Action of the turn still provides the full benefits of the Duration draw next turn. Wharf's Duration effect also increases the card's own reliability by improving the chances that you'll find your villages in time to play them before the next turn's Wharves.
